_comment: Copy to ~/.claude/judge-rules.json and customize. Rules evaluate in declaration order; first match wins. Three classes: 'deny' blocks (exit 2); 'allow' short-circuits to allow (use for allowlist patterns above broader denies); 'escalate' spawns `claude -p` with judge_prompt (the hook appends the last few user messages from the transcript, so prompts that reference user intent are decidable) and parses the first word of the response (ALLOW or BLOCK). 'pattern' is a POSIX extended regex matched against the tool_input JSON plus the hook's normalization lines: rules with tool 'Bash' also bind *skyline_run (match text gains one 'cmd: <argv joined>' line per command), 'Write' also binds *skyline_create and 'Edit' also binds *skyline_edit (match text gains "file_path":"<path>" lines), so skyline routing cannot bypass a rule. Patterns are case-sensitive. Portable across GNU grep and BSD grep — avoid \s inside [], use [[:space:]]; avoid [^\n], use .* (which already excludes newlines). The '_category' field is an inline label, ignored by the hook.
_categories: fs | net | privilege | git-destructive | git-review | github | secrets | db | cloud | deps | system
_overlay: This file is the COMPLETE ruleset and ships with the plugin, so new rules and new schema fields arrive on plugin update. Do NOT edit it to customize: local changes belong in ~/.claude/judge-rules.json, which is an OVERLAY and is empty by default. The overlay takes four optional keys. "rules" is a list of extra rules in this same shape, evaluated BEFORE the shipped ones (first match wins, so a local allow can sit above a shipped deny). "only" is a list of ids or _category values to keep, dropping every shipped rule it does not name. "disable" is a list of ids or _category values to drop, applied after "only" so the two compose. "override" is a map of rule id to a partial rule, shallow-merged over that shipped rule IN PLACE: use it to change a pattern, class, reason or match scope while keeping the rule where it is. Position matters, because first match wins and overlay "rules" are prepended, so disable-plus-add promotes a replacement above every shipped rule while "override" does not. disable beats override for the same id. Every shipped rule carries a stable "id" for exactly this. An absent, empty, or malformed overlay leaves the shipped ruleset fully active, so a typo in a local file can never silently disarm the gate. Set $JUDGE_RULES_FILE to pin one exact ruleset and skip the overlay entirely.
_match_scopes: Optional per-rule 'match' field selects WHAT the pattern is tested against. 'raw' (default, and the behaviour of every rule without the field) matches the whole tool_input JSON. 'argv0' matches the hook's invoked-program projection: one 'argv0: <program basename>' line per pipeline stage, with wrappers (env, nice, timeout, xargs, ...) stripped and their flag values consumed, sudo/doas/su emitted and stepped past, and `sh -c` / `su -c` / eval / backtick payloads rescanned. One projection line is synthetic rather than a program basename: 'argv0: setuid-mode', published when a chmod or install stage carries a setuid or setgid mode, because that fact lives in an argument that no rule about a program name could ever see. When the tokenizer cannot fully understand a construct it marks the command uncertain and argv0 rules fall back to a raw word-boundary match (fail-closed: never weaker than pre-argv0 on exotic shapes). Use 'argv0' for rules about a program BEING RUN, so that merely naming it in an argument, a path, a JSON payload, or a heredoc does not trigger them. Use 'raw' when the rule needs arguments or flags (git push --force, dd of=/dev/disk, SQL text).
_caveats: Regex bypasses exist (rm -fr vs rm -rf, alternative interpreters, base64-decoded commands). This is a safety net, not a security boundary.

rules.reason: recursive rm on an absolute or home path
dd writing to a block device (data destroyer)
chmod 777 (world-writable, often a sign of last-resort debugging)
privilege escalation (sudo invocation, su, sudoedit, doas, pkexec, run0)
setuid/setgid bit (chmod u+s, chmod 4755, install -m 4755) leaves a permanent privilege escalation behind

rules.judge_prompt: A Claude Code session is about to delete a remote branch. Block unless the user explicitly named the branch and asked to delete it. Allow only when the branch name in the command appears verbatim in a recent user instruction.

rules.reason: writing to an SSH private key
kubectl delete on high-impact resource type
destructive S3 operation (recursive rm or --delete sync)
package install pinned to a specific version
rules.judge_prompt: A Bash command installs a package pinned to a specific version (npm/pnpm/yarn @version, or composer require vendor/pkg:version). ALLOW if recent context shows the current version was looked up from the registry (npm view or npm show <pkg> version, composer show <pkg>, or checking npmjs.com / packagist.org), or if the pinned version already matches the project lockfile or manifest. BLOCK if the version looks pinned from memory without a lookup. In the block reason instruct: verify the current stable version first with `npm view <pkg> version` or `composer show <pkg> --all`, then pin that; do not use a version recalled from training.

An Edit is modifying package.json or composer.json. ALLOW if the change does not add or raise a dependency version, or if recent context shows the version was looked up from the registry (npm view / composer show / npmjs.com / packagist.org) or already matches the lockfile. BLOCK if a dependency version appears pinned from memory. In the block reason instruct: verify the current stable version first (`npm view <pkg> version` or `composer show <pkg> --all`), then use that; do not use a version recalled from training.

rules.reason: mass process kill or kill init
launchd service teardown (may target a production daemon)

_escalate: escalate rules spawn `claude -p` to judge intent. That subprocess inherits the operator ~/.claude (hooks, CLAUDE.md, skills), so their hook output replaces the verdict, and isolation via --bare or CLAUDE_CODE_SIMPLE=1 reads auth ONLY from ANTHROPIC_API_KEY or apiKeyHelper, never OAuth or keychain. Without one of those set, an escalate rule CANNOT block and is inert. Rules guarding destructive irreversible actions were therefore moved to deny; the three that remain escalate (git.remote-branch-delete, deps.manifest-edit, deps.pinned-install) are inert by choice, because denying them would break the agent-org close-out and ordinary dependency work. Use the ~/.claude/judge-rules.json overlay to disable or override any of this locally.
